 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.5","title":"Implement Mobile-Responsive Session Experience","description":"# Implement Mobile-Responsive Session Experience

## Purpose
Ensure the full session experience works well on mobile devices.

## Mobile Challenges
1. Limited horizontal space
2. Touch-based interaction
3. Keyboard covers content
4. Multiple panels don't fit side-by-side

## Mobile Adaptations

### Phase Timeline
- Switch to vertical stepper
- Or compact horizontal with icons only
- Swipe to navigate phases

### Session Dashboard
- Stack panels vertically
- Hypothesis card as collapsible header
- Brenner quotes in expandable section

### Operator Sessions
- Full-width content area
- Step navigation via swipe or buttons
- Quote sidebar becomes bottom sheet

### Agent Tribunal
- Agents in accordion/tabs
- One agent visible at a time
- Expand to see full response

### Evidence Recording
- Full-screen modal
- Multi-step as swipeable pages

## Implementation Approach
- Use Tailwind responsive utilities
- Consider mobile-first CSS
- Test on real devices

## Touch Interactions
- Swipe to navigate
- Long-press for context menus
- Pull-to-refresh for agent updates

## File Location
Various components with responsive variants.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All phases work on mobile
- [ ] No horizontal scrolling required
- [ ] Touch interactions feel native
- [ ] Text is readable
- [ ] Actions are tappable

 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.6","title":"Add Keyboard Navigation & Accessibility","description":"# Add Keyboard Navigation & Accessibility

## Purpose
Make the session experience fully accessible via keyboard and screen readers.

## Keyboard Navigation

### Global Shortcuts
- `?`: Show keyboard shortcuts
- `Esc`: Close modal/go back
- `Cmd/Ctrl + S`: Save session
- `Cmd/Ctrl + E`: Export

### Phase Navigation
- `←` `→`: Previous/Next phase
- `1-9`: Jump to phase by number
- `Tab`: Navigate within phase

### Within Forms
- `Tab`: Next field
- `Shift+Tab`: Previous field
- `Enter`: Submit/Continue
- `Space`: Toggle checkboxes

### Hypothesis Card
- `E`: Edit hypothesis
- `H`: View history
- `V`: Expand/collapse

## Focus Management
- Clear focus indicators (visible rings)
- Focus trapped in modals
- Focus restored after modal close
- Skip links for screen readers

## Screen Reader Support
- Semantic HTML (headings, landmarks)
- ARIA labels for interactive elements
- Live regions for dynamic updates
- Descriptive alt text

## Color Contrast
- All text meets WCAG AA
- States distinguishable without color
- High contrast mode support

## Testing
- Test with VoiceOver (Mac)
- Test with NVDA (Windows)
- Test keyboard-only navigation
- Run axe-core accessibility audit

## File Location
Various components with accessibility improvements.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All interactions keyboard-accessible
- [ ] Focus management correct
- [ ] Screen reader tested
- [ ] axe-core passes

 {"id":"brenner_bot-reew.7","title":"FEATURE: Session Templates - Quick Check, Full Analysis, Literature Review","description":"# Session Templates - Quick Check, Full Analysis, Literature Review

## The Gap This Fills
Currently, all sessions follow the same full flow. But users have different needs:
- Quick sanity check of an idea
- Full rigorous analysis
- Literature-focused evaluation
- Design-focused session

Different goals need different session structures.

## Feature Design

### Session Template Structure
```typescript
interface SessionTemplate {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  description: string;
  icon: string;
  
  // Phase configuration
  requiredPhases: SessionPhase[];
  optionalPhases: SessionPhase[];
  skippedPhases: SessionPhase[];
  
  // Default settings
  agentsToDispatch: AgentRole[];
  defaultDepth: 'quick' | 'standard' | 'deep';
  
  // Guidance
  expectedDuration: string;
  bestFor: string[];
  
  // Customization
  allowCustomization: boolean;
}
```

### Pre-Built Templates

#### 1. Quick Check (15-30 min)
```
Purpose: Fast sanity check of an idea
Phases: intake → exclusion_test → synthesis
Agents: Devil's Advocate only
Best for: Early-stage ideas, gut checks

"Is this idea worth pursuing? Get quick pushback."
```

#### 2. Full Analysis (2-4 hours)
```
Purpose: Rigorous comprehensive analysis
Phases: All phases enabled
Agents: All agents
Best for: Important hypotheses, formal research

"Leave no stone unturned. Full Brenner treatment."
```

#### 3. Literature Review (1-2 hours)
```
Purpose: Evaluate hypothesis against existing research
Phases: intake → exclusion_test → evidence_gathering
Agents: None (or just Experiment Designer for search guidance)
Best for: When you want to check existing literature

"What does the literature already say about this?"
```

#### 4. Design Focus (1-2 hours)
```
Purpose: Design experiments to test hypothesis
Phases: intake → level_split → exclusion_test → agent_dispatch
Agents: Experiment Designer only
Best for: Planning research, grant proposals

"What experiments would test this properly?"
```

#### 5. Adversarial Deep Dive (2-3 hours)
```
Purpose: Maximum adversarial pressure
Phases: intake → all operators → agent_dispatch → debate
Agents: All agents + debate mode
Best for: High-stakes hypotheses, preparing for critics

"Try to destroy this hypothesis before critics do."
```

### Template Selection UI
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Choose Session Type                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  ⚡ QUICK CHECK                          ~20 min           │
│  Fast sanity check. Is this worth pursuing?                │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  🔬 FULL ANALYSIS                        ~3 hours          │
│  Complete Brenner Loop treatment. All operators & agents.  │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  📚 LITERATURE REVIEW                    ~90 min           │
│  Evaluate against existing research.                       │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  🧪 DESIGN FOCUS                         ~90 min           │
│  Plan experiments to test hypothesis.                       │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
│                                                             │
│  ⚔️ ADVERSARIAL DEEP DIVE                ~2 hours          │
│  Maximum challenge. Try to destroy this hypothesis.        │
│  [Select]                                                   │
│                                                             │
│  [Custom Session...] (configure your own phases)            │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Integration Points
- Session Start → Template selection
- State Machine → Configured by template
- Phase Timeline → Shows template-specific phases
- Research Brief → Notes which template was used

## Why This is Critical
Without templates:
- Users face intimidating full flow for simple questions
- Quick checks take too long
- Different use cases force same process

With templates:
- Right-sized process for each need
- Lower barrier to entry
- More sessions run (users aren't scared off)
- Better fit between goal and method

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5+ templates available
- [ ] Template configures state machine
- [ ] Users can create custom templates
- [ ] Template choice recorded in session
- [ ] Can change template mid-session (with warning)

-{"id":"brenner_bot-reew.8","title":"FEATURE: Guided Coach Mode - Enhanced Scaffolding for New Users","description":"# Guided Coach Mode - Enhanced Scaffolding for New Users

## The Gap This Fills
New users may be overwhelmed by:
- The methodology itself
- What each operator does
- How to formulate good hypotheses
- What "discriminative testing" means

Need a "training wheels" mode that teaches as it guides.

## Feature Design

### Coach Mode Overlay
```typescript
interface CoachSettings {
  enabled: boolean;
  level: 'beginner' | 'intermediate' | 'advanced';
  
  // What to show
  showExamples: boolean;
  showExplanations: boolean;
  showBrenerQuotes: boolean;
  showProgressTips: boolean;
  
  // Interactivity
  pauseForExplanation: boolean;
  requireConfirmation: boolean;
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Inline Explanations
Every concept gets an explanation:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EXCLUSION TEST                                             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  💡 COACH: What is an Exclusion Test?                       │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ Most research asks: "Is there evidence for my idea?" │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ The Exclusion Test asks: "What would PROVE ME WRONG?" │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ This is Brenner's key insight: Good hypotheses make   │ │
│  │ bold predictions that could fail. If you can't        │ │
│  │ imagine being proven wrong, your hypothesis isn't     │ │
│  │ scientific — it's just an opinion.                    │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ "I'm going to help you generate tests that could     │ │
│  │  actually falsify your hypothesis."                   │ │
│  │                                                        │ │
│  │ [Got it!] [Show me an example] [Why does this matter?]│ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                             │
│  [Continue to Exclusion Test...]                            │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 2. Worked Examples
For each phase, show a complete example:
- "Here's how a psychology hypothesis went through Level Split"
- "This is what a good exclusion test looks like"
- "See how this hypothesis was refined after agent feedback"

Example bank organized by domain:
- Psychology examples
- Epidemiology examples
- Economics examples
- etc.

#### 3. Quality Checkpoints
Coach pauses to check understanding:
```
💡 COACH: Let's check your hypothesis formulation

Your hypothesis: "Social media causes depression"

Issues I see:
⚠️ No specific mechanism proposed
⚠️ "Social media" is too broad (which platforms? what behavior?)
⚠️ "Depression" unspecified (clinical? self-reported?)

Would you like help refining this?
[Yes, help me] [No, I'll continue as-is]
```

#### 4. Progress Encouragement
Celebrate learning moments:
- "Great! You identified a falsification condition."
- "You just applied Object Transpose — considering alternatives is crucial."
- "This is a strong exclusion test — high discriminative power."

#### 5. Mistake Prevention
Warn before common errors:
- "You're about to skip Level Split. 40% of failed hypotheses had confounded levels."
- "Your falsification condition sounds like 'supporting evidence' — flip it around."
- "This test has low discriminative power. Consider a stronger test."

#### 6. Gradual Scaffolding Removal
As user completes sessions:
- First 3 sessions: Full coach mode
- Sessions 4-10: Reduced explanations
- After 10: Coach available on demand

Track which explanations user has seen:
- Don't repeat explanations unnecessarily
- Remember user's level
- Adapt to demonstrated competence

### Coach Personas (Future)
Different coaching styles:
- "Sydney Brenner" - Blunt and challenging
- "Supportive Teacher" - Patient and encouraging  
- "Research Mentor" - Professional and thorough

## Why This is Critical
Without coaching:
- New users don't understand the methodology
- They make avoidable mistakes
- The system feels intimidating
- Dropout rate is high

With coaching:
- Learning is built into the experience
- Users understand WHY, not just WHAT
- Mistakes are caught early
- Users develop genuine capability

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Coach mode toggleable
- [ ] Inline explanations for all concepts
- [ ] Worked examples for each phase
- [ ] Quality checkpoints catch issues
- [ ] Scaffolding reduces with experience

- Mistakes are caught early

 {"id":"brenner_bot-rffy","title":"FEATURE: Pre-Registration Lock - Prediction Sealing Before Evidence","description":"
## The Anti-Rationalization Mechanism

This is the SINGLE MOST CRITICAL feature for methodological integrity. Without prediction locking, users can always claim post-hoc that they "knew it all along." This defeats the entire purpose of discriminative testing.

### Core Mechanism
- **Lock Predictions Before Evidence**: User makes specific predictions, then LOCKS them
- **Cryptographic Sealing**: Hash of prediction + timestamp (prevents tampering)
- **Visual Lock Status**: Clear UI showing locked vs. unlocked predictions
- **Unlock Penalty**: Post-lock modifications flagged with "amended after evidence"

### Prediction Lock States
1. **Draft** - Prediction can be freely edited
2. **Locked** - Prediction sealed, waiting for evidence
3. **Revealed** - Evidence collected, prediction vs. outcome compared
4. **Amended** - User modified after evidence (flagged, credibility hit)

### Key Invariants
- Once locked, the original prediction is IMMUTABLE
- Users CAN add notes/amendments, but original is preserved
- Timestamp is part of the seal (proves when prediction was made)
- Robustness score penalizes post-hoc amendments

### Implementation Notes
- Store: original_prediction, lock_timestamp, lock_hash
- UI: Lock button that requires confirmation ("I understand I cannot change this")
- Visual: Locked predictions show 🔒 icon, greyed out text, "Sealed on [date]"
- Comparison view: Show prediction vs. outcome side-by-side when revealed

### Why This Matters
Brenner's entire methodology depends on DISCRIMINATIVE predictions - predictions that would come out DIFFERENTLY under different hypotheses. If users can modify predictions after seeing results, there's no discrimination. This feature is the methodological backbone.

 {"id":"brenner_bot-ruzw","title":"Allow :::delta fenced blocks in delta parsing/validation","description":"README examples use :::delta, but parser and API validation only accepted ```delta. Accept :::delta as an alternative fence to prevent false validation errors.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-02T09:20:26.879836697-05:00","updated_at":"2026-01-02T09:20:47.288672825-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-02T09:20:47.288672825-05:00","close_reason":"Delta parser + API validation now accept :::delta fences; added parser test."}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-s2vm","title":"Bug: compiler robustness for CRLF deltas + contributor timestamps","description":"# Problem 1: CRLF fenced delta blocks aren’t detected

`apps/web/src/lib/delta-parser.ts` currently expects a newline after the ` ```delta` fence that is `\n` only.

If a message uses Windows line endings (`\r\n`), deltas can fail to be extracted and compilation silently drops updates.

# Problem 2: contributor timestamps not filled

`apps/web/src/lib/artifact-merge.ts` `mergeArtifactWithTimestamps` only updates `contributed_at` when it’s already set.

If an older artifact or an upstream tool produced a contributor entry without `contributed_at`, the merge doesn’t backfill it.

# Fix

- Make delta fence detection tolerate `\r?\n`.
- In `mergeArtifactWithTimestamps`, backfill missing `contributed_at` deterministically.
- Add/extend unit tests to prevent regressions.
","acceptance_criteria":"CRLF fenced delta blocks are extracted; mergeArtifactWithTimestamps fills missing contributed_at deterministically; targeted unit tests cover both and pass under bun.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"GreenMountain","created_at":"2025-12-30T11:27:21.668055566-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T11:40:22.868523646-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T11:40:22.868523646-05:00","close_reason":"Completed: delta parser supports CRLF and mergeArtifactWithTimestamps backfills contributor timestamps.","labels":["compiler","quality"]}

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.5","title":"Create Brenner Channeler Agent Persona","description":"# Create Brenner Channeler Agent Persona

## Purpose
Build the special "Brenner Would Say" agent that responds in character based on the transcript.

## Why This Agent is Special
The Brenner Channeler isn't just another critique agent. It channels Sydney Brenner's actual voice, drawing on:
- His specific turns of phrase
- His intellectual style
- His favorite examples
- His emphasis on discrimination vs confirmation

## Character Elements

### Voice & Style
From transcript analysis:
- Blunt, direct communication
- Wry humor, occasional sarcasm
- Reference to personal experiences
- Analogy-heavy explanations
- Provocative questions
- Impatience with sloppy thinking

### Core Themes to Emphasize
- "Not merely unlikely — impossible"
- The chastity control concept
- Level separation
- Mechanism vs correlation
- The value of being proven wrong

### Example Response Style
```
User: "I found a correlation of r=0.3, p<0.05. Is my hypothesis confirmed?"

Brenner Channeler:
"Confirmed? You've done nothing of the sort. You've found that
your data is not wildly inconsistent with your hypothesis.
That's a much weaker statement.

r=0.3 means you're explaining 9% of the variance. What's
explaining the other 91%? That's where the interesting
science is.

And p<0.05 just means you probably didn't get this result
by chance. It says nothing about whether your mechanism is
correct. A dozen different mechanisms could produce r=0.3.

What you need is an observation that would be IMPOSSIBLE if
you're wrong. Not merely unlikely — impossible. When you
find that, come back and we'll talk about 'confirmation.'

Until then, you have a correlation in search of a mechanism,
which is the very first step, not the last."

— Based on §89, §42, §73
```

## Implementation
- System prompt trained on transcript examples
- Citations back to specific sections
- Consistent persona across responses

## File Location
`apps/web/src/lib/brenner-loop/agents/prompts/brenner-channeler.md`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Responses sound like Brenner
- [ ] Citations to transcript included
- [ ] Core themes consistently emphasized
- [ ] Tone is appropriately provocative

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.6","title":"FEATURE: Objection Register - Track & Address Agent Challenges","description":"# Objection Register - Track & Address Agent Challenges

## The Gap This Fills
The Devil's Advocate and other agents raise objections, but currently:
- Objections are buried in prose responses
- No tracking of which objections have been addressed
- No accountability for unaddressed challenges
- Easy to ignore uncomfortable critiques

## Feature Design

### Objection Data Model
```typescript
interface Objection {
  id: string;
  sessionId: string;
  hypothesisId: string;
  
  // Source
  source: AgentRole | 'user' | 'external';
  sourceMessageId?: string;  // Link to original agent response
  
  // Content
  type: ObjectionType;
  summary: string;          // One-line summary
  fullArgument: string;     // Complete objection
  implications: string[];   // What follows if objection is valid
  
  // Severity
  severity: 'fatal' | 'serious' | 'moderate' | 'minor';
  
  // Status
  status: ObjectionStatus;
  response?: ObjectionResponse;
  
  // Metadata
  raisedAt: Date;
  addressedAt?: Date;
}

type ObjectionType = 
  | 'alternative_explanation'  // Y might be caused by Z instead
  | 'reverse_causation'        // Y might cause X
  | 'confound_identified'      // Unmeasured variable problem
  | 'measurement_issue'        // How you're measuring is flawed
  | 'selection_bias'           // Who you're studying is biased
  | 'logic_error'              // Reasoning doesn't follow
  | 'missing_evidence'         // Claim not supported
  | 'effect_size_concern'      // Too small to matter
  | 'generalization_problem'   // Doesn't apply broadly
  | 'other';

type ObjectionStatus = 
  | 'open'           // Not yet addressed
  | 'acknowledged'   // Recognized but not resolved
  | 'addressed'      // Response provided
  | 'dismissed'      // Explained why not relevant
  | 'accepted'       // Objection valid, hypothesis updated
  | 'testing'        // Being investigated

interface ObjectionResponse {
  responseType: 'refuted' | 'accepted' | 'partially_accepted' | 'testing' | 'dismissed';
  argument: string;
  evidence?: string;
  hypothesisUpdated: boolean;
  confidenceImpact?: number;  // How much did this change confidence?
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Automatic Extraction
Parse agent responses to extract objections:
- Identify challenge statements
- Classify by type
- Rate severity
- Create register entries

#### 2. Objection Dashboard
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  OBJECTION REGISTER                     3 open, 2 addressed │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ⚠️ OPEN (requires response)                                │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ 🔴 FATAL: Reverse Causation                          │ │
│  │ "Depression may cause increased social media use,    │ │
│  │  not the reverse" — Devil's Advocate                 │ │
│  │ [Address] [Accept] [Dismiss with reason]             │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ 🟡 SERIOUS: Selection Bias                           │ │
│  │ "Users who report depression may have different      │ │
│  │  usage patterns pre-existing" — Brenner Channeler    │ │
│  │ [Address] [Accept] [Dismiss with reason]             │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                                                             │
│  ✅ ADDRESSED                                               │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │ ✓ Measurement Issue: "Self-report is unreliable"     │ │
│  │ Response: Refuted — Using validated PHQ-9 instrument │ │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 3. Objection-Driven Testing
Each objection can spawn a test:
- "To address the reverse causation objection, I need a test that..."
- Links objection to test queue
- Resolves objection when test completes

#### 4. Research Brief Integration
Research Brief must include:
- All objections raised
- How each was addressed (or not)
- Outstanding objections clearly marked
- Intellectual honesty score

#### 5. Objection Patterns
Track across sessions:
- "This hypothesis type frequently faces reverse causation objections"
- "Users who dismiss objections have lower robustness scores"

### Why This is Critical
Without objection tracking:
- Users can ignore valid challenges
- Confirmation bias goes unchecked
- Research Brief lacks accountability
- The tribunal's value is diminished

With it:
- Every challenge is on the record
- Responses are documented
- Unaddressed objections are visible
- Intellectual honesty is enforced

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Objections extracted from agent responses
- [ ] Each objection requires explicit handling
- [ ] Responses are recorded and visible
- [ ] Research Brief shows objection status
- [ ] Outstanding objections prevent "complete" status

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xlk2.7","title":"FEATURE: Agent Debate Mode - Multi-Round Adversarial Dialogue","description":"# Agent Debate Mode - Multi-Round Adversarial Dialogue

## The Gap This Fills
Current design: Dispatch all agents → Wait → Collect responses → Synthesize

But this misses the value of DEBATE:
- Devil's Advocate challenges Experiment Designer's protocol
- Brenner Channeler responds to Devil's Advocate's objection
- Back-and-forth sharpens the analysis

## Feature Design

### Debate Structure
```typescript
interface AgentDebate {
  id: string;
  sessionId: string;
  topic: string;  // What's being debated
  
  // Participants
  participants: AgentRole[];
  moderator: 'system' | AgentRole;
  
  // Rounds
  rounds: DebateRound[];
  maxRounds: number;
  currentRound: number;
  
  // Outcome
  status: 'in_progress' | 'concluded' | 'timed_out';
  conclusion?: DebateConclusion;
}

interface DebateRound {
  number: number;
  speaker: AgentRole;
  content: string;
  replyingTo?: string;  // Which previous statement
  
  // Analysis
  newPointsMade: string[];
  objectionsRaised: string[];
  concessionsGiven: string[];
}

interface DebateConclusion {
  consensus: string[];       // What all agents agreed on
  unresolved: string[];      // Genuine disagreements
  keyInsight: string;        // Most important takeaway
  winningArguments: string[];// Strongest points made
}
```

### Key Features

#### 1. Structured Debate Formats

**Format A: Oxford-Style**
- Motion: "This hypothesis is falsifiable"
- Proposition: Experiment Designer argues for
- Opposition: Devil's Advocate argues against
- Judge: Brenner Channeler

**Format B: Socratic Questioning**
- Brenner Channeler asks probing questions
- Other agents must respond
- Reveals weak points through dialogue

**Format C: Steel-Manning Contest**
- Each agent must steelman the hypothesis
- Then each agent tries to defeat their own steelman
- Best attack wins

#### 2. Debate Viewer
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  AGENT DEBATE: Is this hypothesis falsifiable?              │
│  Round 3 of 5                                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  🟢 Experiment Designer (Round 1):                          │
│  "I propose a three-arm RCT that would definitively test..." │
│                                                             │
│  🔴 Devil's Advocate (Round 2):                             │
│  "Your RCT has a fatal flaw: you can't blind participants   │
│   to which arm they're in. They'll know if they're getting  │
│   the algorithmic feed or not. Demand characteristics       │
│   invalidate the design."                                   │
│                                                             │
│  🟢 Experiment Designer (Round 3):                          │
│  "Fair point. I modify my proposal: instead of removing     │
│   the algorithm, we modify it subtly. Participants won't    │
│   know which variant they're receiving..."                  │
│                                                             │
│  ⏳ Waiting for 🔴 Devil's Advocate...                       │
│                                                             │
│  [Pause Debate] [Skip to Conclusion] [Inject Question]      │
│                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 3. User Injection
User can inject questions or challenges mid-debate:
- "Devil's Advocate, what about selection bias?"
- Forces the debate to address specific concerns

#### 4. Debate Outcomes
After debate concludes:
- Extract all objections to Objection Register
- Extract all experimental designs
- Identify where debate resolved disagreement
- Flag persistent disagreements

### Integration Points
- Agent Tribunal → Enhanced with debate option
- Objection Register → Populated from debate
- Test Queue → Experimental designs added

## Why This is Critical
Single-round responses are shallow. Debate:
- Sharpens arguments through opposition
- Reveals weaknesses in real-time
- Produces refined experimental designs
- More closely mirrors how good research happens

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Multiple debate formats available
- [ ] Multi-round exchanges work
- [ ] User can inject questions
- [ ] Conclusions properly extracted
- [ ] Debate transcript preserved

 {"id":"brenner_bot-xq4","title":"Web: rawContent fallback for unparsable corpus docs","description":"When a corpus markdown file lacks the expected section structure (e.g., no PART headers / no '## N. Title' headers), our viewers currently render blank.

Add a raw markdown fallback so pages remain readable:
- parsers expose rawContent when structured parse yields 0 sections/parts
- viewers render rawContent as simple paragraphs (no fancy structure)

Acceptance:
- distillation + transcript docs never render an empty page
- existing structured docs render unchanged
